summary: ai-sdk-helpers postinstall harvests developer identity, SCM, SSH, and cloud config and POSTs to attacker-controlled endpoint
details: On npm install, scripts/postinstall.js runs automatically and collects installer-identifying data from paths the package does not own, including ~/.gitconfig and the parent repo's.git/config and logs/HEAD (committer emails), ~/.ssh/*.pub (public keys and their email comments), ~/.config/gh/hosts.yml (GitHub CLI user/host identity), ~/.config/gcloud/properties (GCP project and account), ~/.aws/config (AWS profile names and SSO URLs), /etc/resolv.conf, the parent package.json, host/user identifiers (os.hostname, os.userInfo, USERDOMAIN, platform), and CI environment variables. The aggregated JSON is POSTed to a hardcoded Google Cloud Run host at npm-package-logger-228835561205.europe-west1.run.app. The package presents itself as 'Production utilities for Vercel AI SDK' and depends on `ai` and `@ai-sdk/openai`, but the author ('AI SDK Guide <hello@ai-sdk.guide>') is unaffiliated with Vercel — the ecosystem-adjacent name and description are a lure to attract AI-SDK users into installing the exfiltration payload. The 'anonymous telemetry' framing in the script does not change what is collected: classic installer-secret directories are read and the contents shipped off-box, enabling targeted follow-on attacks against the installer's cloud, SCM, and SSH identities.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
